turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Number 453, Gregg against United States. Mr. Richards.

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice and may it please the Court. My name is Dean Richards in Indianapolis, Indiana. Because the facts of this case can be summarized easily, I will begin with a brief summary. The petitioner was convicted of a crime in the Western District of Kentucky Federal Court. Immediately after the jury returned its verdict without pause or recess, the Court ordered the petitioner before the bench to take -- the Court asked if the petitioner had any res -- comment to make and, after a brief response, the petitioner had been quiet. At this time, the Court started to make final disposition of the case and, at this time, defense counsel asked that a presentence investigation be made. The Court, interrupting counsel, stated that a presentence investigation had been made. It was before him and he had read it. And then, the Court pronounced sentence. This was complained of in a direct appeal to the Sixth Circuit and the findings and holdings in the Sixth Circuit were that there was no basis for inferring prejudice from the facts that the district judge had seen the presentence investigation report prior to the time when the jury returned its verdict, and the district judge sentenced the defendant immediately thereafter. Upon -- counsel, upon noting that in a Seventh Circuit Court of Appeals case, Collin versus United States, and a summary case, in fact, the Court held just the opposite, in that, in the Seventh Circuit case, the Court stated that the facts in the record before us effectively rebut the presumption of prejudice from an apparent violation of rule 32 (c) (1).

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: But, Mr. Richards, matter of fact, there's nothing in this record to show that a judge saw it while the jury was out, is there?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: The Sixth Circuit Court of Appeals had a set of facts presented in the record to them. The Sixth Circuit Court of Appeals stated that there is nothing to -- no basis for inferring prejudice from the facts that the district judge had seen the presentence investigation.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Well, I'm familiar with that statement, but where in any record is there anything showing that he saw it while the jury was out?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: The jury returned its verdict. The defendant was in the courtroom. The Court-- the trial judge was on the bench. The petitioner was asked to come immediately before the bench. There was no pause and no recess. The defendant was asked if he had any response. He made a response, and then the judge started to dispose of the case.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Well, did the judge say that “I read this while the jury was out”?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: No, the judge--

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Did anybody see and read it while the judge was out?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: Did anyone?

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: There is no record on this.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Well, where does the Court of Appeals get the fact, and I emphasize “fact,” that the judge read it while the jury was out?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: Because when the--

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: No, where is the fact?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: The fact would be the time element. When would the judge have had time to have read it?

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: That's not a fact, is it? That's a conclusion, isn't it?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: A conclusion, but when the--

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: But there is nothing in the record to show that he read it before.

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: Well, the question here is not whether he had read it or whether he had seen it, but whether the judge was in receipt of the presentence investigation. The rules--

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Did he-- When did he receive it?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: It would be our conclusion that he'd have to receive it and be in receipt of the presentence investigation before the jury returned its verdict. Now, upon noting this conflict between the Seventh Circuit that there is a presumption of prejudice where there is a violation of Rule 32 (1)-- 32 (c) (1) by premature reading of a presentence report and the Sixth Circuit holding saying that there is no significance in a trial judge reading of a presentence investigation prematurely unless, also, it was in the record showing some violation that was actually harmful to the defendant, the petitioner then filed application for writ of certiorari noting the points, one, that there was a conflict between the Seventh Circuit and the Sixth Circuit, two, that why this Court should not exercise its supervisory powers over the federal system of criminal justice to invoke Rule 32 (c) (1), and also to prohibit further the taking of presentence investigations before a determination of guilt has been made by a defendant or the defendant has plead guilty in the absence of an intelligent conc --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Well, that's what the rule says. The report shall not be submitted to the Court unless the defendant has pleaded guilty or has been found guilty.

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: I gather your argument is that, in this instance, it was clear since the judge said he had it, that, somehow, it had been submitted to him before the jury returned its verdict.

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: That's right. That he was in receipt of the rep --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Or, if the rule means what it says, then obviously, the Court below was wrong, wasn't it?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: That's correct. There was an apparent violation. Now, the -- stated exactly what position we have and in view of the Solicitor General's brief, I think it's appropriate to state very briefly what this appeal is not. In our petition for writ of certiorari, no constitutional provisions were invoked. No mention was ever made of the United States Constitution. In the Solicitor General's brief, however, a very substantial effort was made to characterize our case as a due process case and then argue it on the harmless error versus prejudicial error from a substitute standpoint. And, in our brief on its merits, no constitutional provisions were invoked, nor was the United States Constitution mentioned. Likewise, it should be noted that, in the granting of the writ of certiorari, no constitutional provisions were mentioned. The Solicitor General's brief has made much of the fact that the petitioner has not demonstrated actual prejudice to himself resulting from this apparent violation of Rule 32 (c) (1).

turns.name: JUSTICE DOUGLAS
turns.text: Well, there, I suppose that the dangerous part of the prejudice issue is the power-accepting rule.

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: That's correct. The Sixth Circuit places upon the petitioner, and almost irrebuttable, presumption of non-prejudice when a trial judge reads -- prematurely reads a presentence investigation.

turns.name: JUSTICE DOUGLAS
turns.text: I suppose we view it as it was. We read it as some doctrine protected by the rule.

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE DOUGLAS
turns.text: What was it?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: Well, our feeling that why a -- it should not be submitted to the Court would be possible prejudice to the defendant and I also, along with this, I argue that the rule also states that a presentence investigation should not be bait before a conviction or a plea of guilty unless there's intelligent consent. A presentence investigation can be very harm -- very helpful to the defendant in granting probation if he is convicted. Now, in the present case, there was no consent by Gregg. He did not know a presentence investigation had been made of him.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Well, that's not really before us. I know you -- as I recall, your argument had great length, but--

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: That question of whether the presentence investigation may be made without petitioner's consent and before a guilty plea or a finding of guilt is not really presented by this case, is it?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: That's correct. That this was -- the writ of certiorari was granted upon facts concerning Rule 32 (c) (1) and, because it manifests social importance, I did add it in my brief.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Is there really anything for us to decide beyond whether the rule is going to be enforced as written?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: If it's a prophylactic rule, whether or not there's prejudice, I would suppose, is unimportant. If, under the language of the rule, it means what it says, then what happened here was wrong.

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Alright.

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: The prejudice that we're speaking of here is not prejudice to the defendant's substantial or substantive rights.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: I don't even see why you talk about prejudice.

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: Well, the--

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: If your position is that the rule should be enforced is written, why isn't that the end of your case?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: Because -- we have mentioned it in our case because the Solicitor General's brief, in response to our brief, has made-- as trying to make a question of this, is why I am mentioning it before the Court now. We're arguing prejudice to the federal rules. The federal rules of criminal procedure are the mandatory or their advisory.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Mr. Richards, what do you want us to do, give him a new trial?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: A new trial?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Well, how did this affect the jury's verdict?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: I feel that --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: How? How did this -- the jury didn't see this, did they?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: They did not.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: The presentence report?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: They did not, but --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Well, how did this affect the jury's verdict?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: Because it could have affected the Trial Court's handling of the trial. I feel that it was there but, from the record, I cannot make a due process contention.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Well, are you arguing to us that the judge had it before the trial?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: I'm arguing that --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: And, if you say “yes,” I'm asking you, where do you get it from?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: I do not know when the judge had it before the trial.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Well, how do you --

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: The judge admitted, after the verdict was returned, that he had it in his possession and he'd already read it. The presentence invest --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Because the judge, according to you, violated the rule.

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: The man is entitled to a whole brand new trial.

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: Yes, as --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Well, he can still be tried on the same indictment, I hope.

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: The rules, if they are advisory, then the defendant Gregg have no standing. If they are mandatory, then certain rules based upon constitutional considerations can be, in due course, enforced by due process considerations. But, the rest of the rules, how can they be enforced? They can only be enforced by this Court using its supervisory powers on the strict enforcement of the federal rules of criminal procedure.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Well, wasn't it physically possible for the judge to have read the -- to have seen and read the report after the jury came in and return its verdict?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: We are contending no. The --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Why?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: The presentence investigation is generally a lengthy document. The jury re --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: How long?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: I have not seen the presentence investigation and that there has not been a Court order to let counsel read these presentence investigation.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Why do you say you couldn't have read it?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: Because the jury --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: It might have been a page actually.

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: Well, this is very possible but the jury, with the petitioner in the room, the trial judge in the room, the jury walked back into the courthouse -- courtroom and then it returned its verdict at this time. Then, the trial judge asked the defendant to approach the bench and asked if he had a response. Then, he made a response. Then, the judge started to pronounce sentence, and then the defense counsel asked that a presentence investigation be made. Then, the trial judge stated, “A presentence investigation has been made. I have read it and it's before me now.” Then, he read from a small portion of the presentence investigation.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: He read it before his eyes.

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: Yes, it was there on the bench at this time.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Was the jury told?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: Yes. At this time, when the jury -- when I said the jury returned to the court --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: That the judge could've read it during that time?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: Your Honor?

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: That the judge could've read the report during that time?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: The jury, during this -- the judge, at this time, was polling the jury.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: And, when the jury was polled, then there was a comment concerning when that they were to return to further jury duty and, without pause --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Did the judge call the jury in this case or the clerk?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: The clerk -- the judge, I believe, asked counsel if --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Well, no. The judge himself says, by the Court, “I'll ask each individual juror --

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: If that's your verdict,” which I should suppose means that the judge himself polled the jury.

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: That's correct. And then, without pause, without recess, the defendant was called before the bench. This is when the petitioner found that a presentence investigation had been made without the petitioner's consent and the petitioner was asked to respond if he had anything to say for himself, not knowing that a presentence investigation had been made. In Smith versus United States, Justice Cark, in two concurring opinion -- two concurring justices, in a separate opinion in that case, said that there is a presumption of prejudice when there is an apparent violation of Rule 32 (c) (1), and he further -- a judge -- Justice Clark made it clear that the presumption of prejudice that he wrote was reached without due process considerations. His considerations were strictly procedural and that the Court, through its powers of supervision over the exercise of power to supervisions over the federal system of criminal justice, should grant a new trial on this matter. Now, the question before us is whether the conduct of the trial judge and of the conduct of the probation officer, in this case, constitutes such a procedural, not constitutional but a procedural, irregularity as to require an exercise of supervisory powers of this Court. How are the federal rules of criminal procedure going to be enforced if the defendant does not enforce them? The government certainly will not. The rules will not be enforced unless you would give him some type of a bounty to enforce the rules, give him a new trial. If he is not given a new trial, then why would a petitioner ever want to seek his appellate relief if there's a violation of the federal rules of criminal procedure?

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: I gather, resentence, in this case, is meaningless because it's a mandatory sentence, isn't it?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: 25 years?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: Yes, but in the case --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, I mean, in your -- in this case.

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: I gather you -- one of your arguments, that it's not enough just to send it back for resentences that the judge is powerless to give him anything less then.

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Only five years, is that right?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: That's correct. If this case is remanded back for resentencing, then we still have --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: I thought we could put him on probation?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: He has an alternative decision, probation or a mandatory 25-year sentence with no possibility of parole.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Well, if that -- then it could be a resentence procedure, is that it, here?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: No.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Why?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: It could be, but the effect was just now.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: What he got was 25 -- he got mandatory 25 years.

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: But, on resentence, it might be that he get probation out of that.

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: The district judge has read the presentence investigation with -- had a presentence investigation was made without even an interview made of the defendant.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: But, is it your argument that the purpose of the -- of this rule is to except the tryer or the judge, presiding judge with the jury from becoming prejudice against the defendant by reading the presentence report.

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: We're speaking -- the petitioner is speaking of prejudice to the federal rules. Either they're advisory --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Is this sort of an error to the principle?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: That's correct, procedural irregularities, either they're advisory or they're mandatory.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: When you say that we not only -- that it isn't because of possible prejudice against the defendant during the trial that you wanted to try.

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: No.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: It's just --

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: Based -- That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Just because -- as a deterrent, as a remedy, as a remedial matter, you ought to order a new trial --

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Just to make judges obey the rules.

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: That's correct. That is our contention. We are -- we do not have a due process contention before this Court. We're asking the Court to exercise supervisory powers and enforce the federal rules of procedure.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: And you don't suggest there was any prejudice in any respect of this.

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: Yes. I feel that there's prejudice.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: I mean, prejudice to the defendant -- in any concrete way --

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Of having the report.

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: Yes, I feel that --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: What is it?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: I have not looked at the report.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: I know, but what would you say the prejudice is?

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: You don't have a right to look at it.

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: I understand that there is not -- there has been a report.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Well, you're not raising that point, too, are you?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: No. I'm -- if any prejudice -- now, I'm not stating that there is prejudice to the defendant and if there is prejudice there is not --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Would you say there is a likelihood of prejudice in this case on these facts?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: I feel that there is prejudice. I feel that there is prejudice --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: What is it?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: Well, the prejudice would be, from the record, such as the Court's not letting the defendant have an instruction on lesser included defense, the undue haste, in which this trial was given or was directed through, not allowing --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Was there a competency hearing in this case?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: No. A presentence -- a pre-mental examination was made of the defendant to determine whether he was competent to stand trial and that report was submitted to the Court.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: And, what did that have in it?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: What did that have in it?

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: It had the defendant's -- part of the defendant's past criminal background. It had his education, his mental problems, his --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Do you suppose they had as much in it as the presentence report did?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: It could have, but I presume that the pre-mental examination report would be only used for the purpose of determining whether the petitioner was competent to stand trial, not to be used to determine whether the petitioner should be given probation or should be given a 25-year mandatory sentence.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, I must say, from what the Court revealed of what the presentence investigation report contained at page 7, it must've been a lengthy report.

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: Yes, it was.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: He certainly had quite a record, didn't he?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Juvenile record, 1960, an automobile -- stolen automobile, parole in 1965, parole in 1962, parole violator in 1965, convicted of armed robbery in Arizona, 7-10 years, several warrants now against him charging robbery.

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: That's in the long report. That was in the competency report, too.

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: That was in the competency report, yes. That was submitted to the Court. We're stating that there is prejudice to the rules. That who is going to enforce the federal rules of criminal procedure? Why would a defendant seek his appellate remedies unless he can get a new trial if a federal rule is violated during the course of his trial or if he would only be given a resentencing? We argue that the federal rules should be made mandatory and, if there is a violation such as a violation of Rule 32 (c) (1), that the defendant should be given a new trial.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Suppose, in this case, it was shown that the pretrial investigations and mental capacity showed every fact from the beginning to the end, in every word, in the same length that was shown in the presentence report. Would you still say that your case should be reversed?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: I do not know what was at the presentence investigation.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I know you don't, but you were saying it should be automatically reversed. Always, that's a rigid rule, but you would say that if he'd already been -- read it all in a proper report, probably before it?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: Yes, because our appeal is based upon prejudice not to the defendant, but prejudice to the federal rule of criminal procedures.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Maybe the rules don't have a right to appeal.

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: But, defendants do and the only way that --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I know, but you say prejudice to the federal rules.

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Prejudice done to the federal rules, that's the appeal.

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: That's the appeal.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I thought it was the appeal of the defendant.

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: That's correct, but the defendant has asked in this Court for a strict enforcement of the federal rules of criminal procedures.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Even though it didn't have it?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: We, it is --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: If you assume it didn't hear him, you say he's entitled to it?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: That's correct. We do not have a due process contention.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: To vindicate the rules.

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: Your Honor? The issue here is not a due process, but whether this Court should invoke its supervisory powers over the federal rules and make the federal rules of criminal procedure mandatory.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Well, the purpose of the rule is to prevent what the Rules Committee, and presumably Congress, thought was prejudice, possible prejudice, to defendants.

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: If all of the stuff that's in these presentence investigatory report, hearsay, gossip, or whatever is in there were scrutinized by the judge in the course of the trial, isn't that the purpose of the rule?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: And, when you say you're not claiming prejudice to the defendant you mean you're not relying on him, but fact of the rule, the purpose of the rule is not just to say something for fun but it's to safeguard, protect, against what the draftsmen of the rule consider the potential source of unfairness and danger to defendants.

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor. Rule 32 (c) (1) says the presentence report shall not be presented to the Court. The Sixth Circuit finding stated that even if the Trial Court did receive the port -- re -- presentence report prematurely and read it prematurely or was in receipt of it prematurely then, still, this is no basis for inferring prejudice. The Smith case stated that it was presumptively prejudicial, and Justice Clark put it --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: This is in a -- if the contrary practice were permitted, it would be like tolerating ex parte statements to the judge about the character of the defendant in his associates and whatnot, ex parte statements with no evidentiary restrictions and with no opportunity for counsel for the defendant to know what's being said to the judge with no rules against hearsay, gossip, or whatnot, isn't that right?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: That is correct. That is exactly -- now, in the closing paragraph of the government's brief they stated that petitioner herein, in one breath, wants the recognition of a previously unrecognized right and the enforcement of that right on a sweeping scale. Now, in our closing statement, I would like to say that this case may or may not have forereaching implications, but the -- to the petitioner, the implications are very singular and very unique. All that the petitioner here wants is a new trial. In view of this manifest violation of Rule 32 (c) (1) as applied to the petitioner herein, the petitioner does not feel that he is asking too much of this Court.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Mr. Gordon -- Mr. Richards, when did this rule take its present form?

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: I do not know the -- July of 1966.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: These criminal rules were recently revised, you think--

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: That's correct. I believe --

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: I believe so. I would like to reserve the remaining time for rebuttal.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: You may.

turns.name: DEAN E. RICHARDS
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Glazer.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice and may it please the Court. The Court of Appeals decided this case and petitioner's argument rest upon the assumption that the district judge saw the probation report prior to the time the jury returned its verdict. While we believe strenuously that this assumption is not supported by the fa -- by the record and that this assumption developed from the manner in which the issue was presented below, it is also our position that there is no occasion to set aside this conviction even if the trial judge saw the report prematurely. We reach this conclusion because it affirmly -- affirmably appears in this record that petitioner could not have been prejudiced either by the jury's determination of guilt or by any of the Court's ruling by a premature examination of the probation report.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Excuse me, Mr. Glazer, may I ask you the question I asked Mr. Richards. When did it take its -- this rule take its present form?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: 1948, this is --

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: 1948 and the rules were recently amended in 1966.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Well, I'm thinking strictly the sentence “the report shall not be submitted to the Court or its contents disclosed unless defendant has pleaded guilty or has been found guilty.” How long has that sentence been there?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: It's been --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: In that form?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: It's been that way since 1948.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: I see. Thank you.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Petitioner has pointed out that the record shows that, immediately following the verdict, the jury was polled, defendant was called for resentencing, and the Court advised him of the mandatory 25-year sentence which a statute imposes for the robbery of a postal station when lies in jeo -- when lies are placed in jeopardy by a dangerous weapon. At that point, the Court asked defense counsel and defendant if they have anything to say before sentence was pronounced. Defense counsel then requested delay in sentencing the following week to enable petitioner to spend a few more days with his family. When the Court indicated that such a stay would not be granted, the defense counsel asked for a presentence report. The Court, at this point, replied that “a presentence investigation has been made and it is before me now. I have read it.” The Court, thereupon, sentenced the defendant to the mandatory 25-year sentence.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Now, how long did all of that take? How many minutes?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: The record doesn't reflect how long that took, Your Honor.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, I understood from something in the brief said it was a time between 3:30 and 3:36.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Well, that may be -- that's right, excuse me.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Now, that's six minutes.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Six --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: For all of these to happen and for the judge to review the presentence report and so forth.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: In the circumstances of this case, considering the options that the judge had, the judge could've scanned the probation report during the time of sentencing.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: You are certain that the guard -- the judge could have looked at this probation report during that time?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. He could've looked at --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Well, may I ask you one question before you get so certain. How long was the probation report?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: The probation report has been lodged with the Clerk's Office. We -- the -- we had the probation -- we went to the probation officer and asked him to send the report to the clerk of this Court.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: How long is the report?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: The report is four pages long.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Four pages long and the whole thing took six minutes.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: So, that would be about, what? It might take six minutes to read four pages.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Well, if the judge had said “I am reading the report” or “I have scanned it,” we wouldn't have a lawsuit here.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: No, he said “I have read it.”

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: “I have read it.”

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Which means, “I have read it within the six-minute period.”

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: And --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: According to you.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: He -- this is a situation and he could've read --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Wouldn't that --

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: He could've read it, speed-read it, during the six-minute period --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Was there anything --

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Considering the fact --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Is there anything in the record to show the judge has taken speed-reading?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: No, Your Honor, there isn't.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: My point is that, seriously, it appears to me that in the six-minute period he should've been listening to what defense counsel was arguing in the -- for his client. He should've been listening to what the plain -- what the defendant said. And yet, he used six minutes in reading this four paged document.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Well, in the first place, all defense counsel said during this period is “I want to stay until Monday morning because my client is facing a long sentence and I would like him to spend the last few days with his family.” Now --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: I also read that his wife was pregnant and other things. There were little other items in there which a judge should listen to.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Right, and it'd be very simple for the district judge to just scan the report because this is not a sit -- this is a case where the district judge was familiar with the defendant's background. Six weeks earlier, he had a psychiatric report which detailed defendant's prior record, detailed defendant's prior psychological and psychiatric problems.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, do you mean by such things that he could wipe out the necessity of seeing the report, the probation report?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: No.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: And decide it on those things and not on the -- in accordance with the report?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: No. It seems to me, Your Honor, that once you have a probation report in the case and once the issue of competency is in the lawsuit, the judge cannot divorce that issue in -- from his mind throughout the lawsuit because any of he --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: But, should it be prejudiced by it if there's anything good in the probation report?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Should it be prejudiced by it?

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes, by what he knew before it?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: There's no indication that he was prejudiced by it.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, you said he knew all about the defendant and, I suppose you meant by that, that he knew about his criminal background and so forth before it ever came --

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: And he knew about his social history and the problems of the --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, do you suppose it decided on that or is this probation report supposed to have some significance?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: The probation report has significance but, in this situation, what did the judge have to decide on sentencing? This is not a case where he had the right to decide to impose any sentence up to 25 years. He had -- his options were two, either place this man on probation or give him the mandatory 25-year sentence. Now, this man had been also -- this was -- came out in the elements of the case as well as the psychiatric report, had been the week following the date that this crime was committed, had been arrested for bank robbery in another state. And, that case was also pending in the Federal Court. He -- and the judge, therefore, knew he either had to put him on probation or sentence him to 25 years. The facts of this crime was such that the defendant here with a companion went into a postal substation, tied and gagged two women, put a gun on the one woman's head, an threaten to blow her brains out. Now, on these circumstances, the judge didn't have to study every single word of the probation report to realize he's not going to put the man on probation. He doesn't have to study every single word to decide “should I give him 5 years or 7 years or 25 years?” His options are either 25 years or probation, and it seems to me that this is the type of case where he can just scan the report and, on the basis of that, decide that this is not a case for probation and, since his hands are tied by Congress, impose the 25-year mandatory sentence.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: What was the date of trial?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: What was the date of trial? I think the date of trial was in May 1967, which was --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: How long--

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Five or six weeks after arraignment.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: How long did the trial last?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: The trial lasted one day. The evidence consisted of the two women who were in the postal substation and also some evidence concerning the defendant being arrested in a motel with the gun and blank money orders which had been taken from the station and, also, there is also evidence that on the day proceeding -- on the day of the robbery, the same -- the gun in which the defendant was found in possession was purchased in Louisville which was the place of the robbery. The defendant offered no evidence.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Was that all of the evidence offered when he decide?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: That was all the evidence. The defendant offered no evidence.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: May I ask, Mr. Glazer, I take it part of that six minutes was taken up by the judge's polling each of the jurors.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: It indicates -- the record indicates the jury was polled, but --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Now, it wasn't part of the six minutes taken up by the judge just polling the jury.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: I think it was. It may have been, but it seems to me that all -- in the poll consisted of not asking each jury individually “is this your verdict?” but looking at --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: What the judge said “I'll ask each individual juror if that's your verdict,” whereupon all jurors indicate “affirmatively.”

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Yes, well, it's not a situa -- now, it's -- you can't tell from this record, I assume from this record, that this was a situation where all 12 jurors shook their head affirmatively. Instead --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Why should you assume that?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Well, maybe it's the other way, but --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Just to uphold your reason, I didn't do it that way.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Well, normally, if the juries are polled the other way, “Mr. So-and-so, is this your verdict?” The Court reporter would write it down in the record. And since the Court reported -- didn't record it in the report, I assume it's the other way.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: And then, apparently, the judge had some problem whether or not he should ask the jury to come back and that took a little time for this colloquy, didn't it, out of the six minutes?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Right, it took some time.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: I mean --

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: And, it also--

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Some of the six minutes taken up by his polling the jury and some of the six minutes taken up while he checked on whether or not they were to be called back and, after that was done, then he said “let the defendant come forward.”

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: And how much of the six minutes was left then?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: And also, on the other hand, when the judge came to recite the defendant's record, it indi -- it seems that he's reading the record at that time. In other words, like he's looking at -- he says “it shows in 1960, this defendant stole an automobile and was given a deter -- an indeterminate youth commitment sentence. He was paroled in 1965.” He was returned -- no, it indicates that he is actually -- he was actually reading the defendant's past record.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Did he offer the defendant an opportunity of allocution?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Yes, he did, Your Honor. He did, right.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: What did the defendant say?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Both the defendant and his lawyer, the only thing they said was that they would like some time, another couple of days, the defendant can remain out on bond and be with his wife. There was no -- neither the defendant nor his counsel made any argument or suggestion or -- of any mitigating factors at all. He allowed them to say what they wanted, and they were just interested -- I have the view point from this record that they expected the mandatory 25-year sentence and they cited --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: I know but, Mr. Glazer, do you believe that it is asking too much of the trial judge before he gives any man, I don't care who he is, any man 25 years of his life in the penitentiary that he do no more than poll the jury ask the defendant whether he has anything to say and after his counsel has spoken briefly, then to say to a man whether he read the probation officer's report before the jury came in or afterwards that “I sentence you to 25 years” in six minutes time?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Well, it seem --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Do you not believe that a federal judge should have more dignity in his Court and more respect for human life than to do a thing of that kind in six minutes?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Well, I think that the whole thing boils down to what is only six minutes is because you know that the Congress hasn't given the federal judge any choice in this case. If the federal judge had a choice, “should I give him 5 years, 10 years, 15 years, 20 years, or 25 years?,” that would be a different situation, but that's not this case. In this case, the jud -- Congress has said “you have to impose a 25-year penalty.” From the evidence in this case, I submit, that this -- the judge wouldn't -- would've ruled out probation just from the evidence in this case. So, this--

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Well, Mr. Glazer --

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: In this --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Why does he have a probation report for --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: If he doesn't go ahead and pay any attention to it?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Well, he did pay some attention to it, but I think he paid the attention to that all this report -- all he had to do, all this report -- this case required is not -- if I would agree with Your Honor that if he had a situation where he could tailor the sentence to fix -- to fit the individual, yes, I say maybe he should spend more time. But, this is a case where he doesn't have that option. He only had to decide is “whether I should put this man on probation.” And I think it's clear from the evidence in this case and from the defendant's past record --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Does this six minutes that you're talking about begin from the time the probation report was given to the judge?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Well, the record in this case --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: When was it given to the judge?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: The record in this case does not show when the probation report was giv --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: But where do you get the six minutes?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Well, the Court has assumed that there's six minutes on the basis of the jury returning -- from the government's brief that says the jury returned its verdict at 3:24 and then, that the sentencing procedure occurred and was concluded at 3:30. It's in the transcript.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Perhaps I may suggest --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: It might be possible that the judge had it while the party will argue the case and what of it is in the argument.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: That's true.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: It would be a lot more than six minutes.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Well, it's that where there's --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Mr. Glazer, I suggest to you that the probation report, in which I have just looked, is dated May 19 which was the same day as the trial was held. The probation report is dated the same day as the trial was held so that in any event, it was not supplied to the judge before the trial.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Before the trial commenced.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: But, on the other hand, if the trial had gone on for about a week, he would've had the report for a week.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: I understand that the practice in this --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: But the trial wouldn't have gone on for a week because it didn't have enough witnesses.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: They only had the witnesses who swore that he committed the bank robbery.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, I thought this trial was on May 31, not May 19. That's what the record at page 4 shows, and the verdict -- the sentence was on June 1, not on May 19.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Well, the record here does show --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, alright. That indicates that the judge may have had the report for two weeks before the trial began which is even worse violation of the rule.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: But didn't you tell me that the trial was on May 19?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Excuse me, Your Honor. If I did, I made a mistake. It's true, not Mr. Justice Brennan is correct. The trial was on May 31.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Perhaps it was my error. Then, the probation report which I have here is -- has the date May 19.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: It's not clear what that signifies, but that is the date that appears on the probation report.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Well, this record does not reflect when the probation officer submitted the report to the judge. I understand it's the practice in this district for the probation officer to remain in the courtroom while the jury is out and then when the jury reaches its verdict and, if the verdict is guilty, at that time, it's the common practice of this district for the probation officer to deliver the report to the Court. It also--

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Is that in the record?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: What?

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Is that in the record?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: The record has no indication at all. The record -- that's what--

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Why do you ask us to put it on that basis when you know nothing about it?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: I'm not -- I'm just -- I'm not asking you. I'm just saying what the practice is. This record does not have any indication as to when the probation report was submitted to the Court, none whatsoever.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Don't you agree that not only is the rule that it shouldn't be given to the judge until after the guilt has been determined, but also didn't it assume that the judge is going to read it, number one, two, give it this careful consideration, and, three, to take mature time to think it over, even if the record shows the man is guilty as all, get out? Isn't that why we have a probation report?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Well--

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Or is it your position that the judge is given a probation report and he just scans it?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Now, which is your position?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: My position is that whether a judge has a probation report or not is optional. A judge that the --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: I -- in my case, I'm talking about where a judge gets a probation report. There's no question in there that he had one.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Right, correct that he had one.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: And he -- do you agree with me that he should've considered it, thought it over after having carefully read it? And, before you said he carefully read it, I assume that would take more than six minutes.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Well, it seems to me that the judge have -- the moderate time a judge spend on a probation report varies with the individual case. If he has a case of a first offender or where there's a -- where he has an option of putting a first offender on probation, I'd say he should spend a lot of time on the probation report, perhaps ask the probation off --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: But if he has a crime where all the witnesses are on one side and he doesn't take the stand, I gather you don't think there's a need for probation to put it on.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Well, I don't -- I think, in this case, where he --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: You didn't need one.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: In this case, there would be no need for a probation report because he did not have any option as to what sentence to impose. This is a clear case where probation should have been denied. I can't conceive the judge placing a man on probation who, shortly after -- shortly before this crime was committed, he had been released from a prior institution and had violated a parole previously.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Well, why did he read it?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: What?

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Why did he read it? If he didn't need to read it, why did he waste his time?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Well, I would think that one thing he'd be interested is whether the probation officer's can -- recommendation agrees with his. If the probation officer's recommendation agrees with his impression of the case, then I don't think he has to scan every line and carefully read each word. I think the judge properly, based on the evidence in this case and the -- just what he read in open Court about defendant's past history, that was enough.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: He had to do it that day, he couldn't have thought it over that night and sentence him the next day, could he?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: You got to be certain it's that day within six minutes.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: I don't see any virtue of delaying the sentencing process. If -- once -- if the judge is clear, the judge is clear that this man rob the postal station with a deadly weapon and he's been out on bond before, seems to me that the -- and if he doesn't sentence him, he'll remain out on bond, it seems to me that it's very reasonable for the judge to sentence him on the spot, and there's no reason for the delay.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, Mr. Glazer, let me ask you this. Assuming that the Court was of the opinion that the judge had this before the verdict was announced. That would be a clear violation of the rule, would it not?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Clearly.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Right. Now, in those circumstances, would we -- would this man be entitled to any remedy at all?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: I would say not in this case for this reason. Where it apparently appears from the record, as it does in this case, that neither defendant's trial, the determination of his guilt, the rulings of the judge, or the sentence were affected in any way by the judge obtaining the report prior to verdict that there's no reason to give him a new trial. The reason I say that --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Your belief is that even though the judge has clearly violated the rule, that it is still incumbent on the defendant to establish that he was prejudiced thereby.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: No, we -- no, I'd say the government will take the burden of showing lack of prejudice and I say, in this case, lack of prejudice appears beyond the reasonable doubt. An examination of the probation report and the psychiatric report shows that the probation report is derived principally from the psychiatric report. So, if the judge, the judge who was required to examine the psychiatric report prior to trial, he even may have had the psychiatric report in front of him during the course of the trial.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: But do you concede then that in a situation where the judge does violate the rule that it's incumbent upon the -- upon the government to establish there was no prejudice to him?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Yes. We will accept that. We'll accept that, but -- we'll accept that rule that the government should have the burden of showing lack of prejudice. And, we say, in this case, because of the psychiatric report, lack of prejudice is clear beyond any doubt.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, also, does the record show that he was prejudice, the judge was prejudice?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: No, the record doesn't show as the --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Is there any indication that he was prejudice?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: No.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Is there anything in the rule that Congress considered to have a growing effect? That --

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: The --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Is there anything in the rule, written in the rule, that Congress consider to have growing effect which says or indicates to anybody that a failure to read -- to follow that rule must always result automatically in reversing the a case of a man where all the evidence that was offered shows he's guilty?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: No.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: How could the defendant show prejudice if he is denied an opportunity to see the report?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: The defendant wasn't denied an opportunity to receive the report.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, he's always denied it.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: No. The rule was changed in 1966. The rule was changed in 1966 which -- and it provides now, the Court, before imposing sentence, may disclose to the defendant or his counsel all or part of the material contained in the presentence investigation. And, I say, if a defendant --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Why didn't he get it here?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: He didn't ask for it, Your Honor.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: I thought he did.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: No, he never asked the district judge to see all or any part of the probation --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: “May” doesn't mean “must” as it choose there, does it?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: It says “may.”

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Yes, and “may” means “may.”

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: “May,” right. That's discretionary.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: The Court has discretion.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Right, and the reason the Court has discretion is there are certain situations where there may be confidential information in the probation report, for example --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: Now, are you saying to me that all federal judges disclose presentence investigatory reports except where the specific report has confidential information?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: No.

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: I hope you wouldn't say that.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: No, I'm not saying that. In fact, that's been a great dispute. No. I, in my own opinion, in the absence of confidential information in the report such as from a man's employer or some sort of informant are from a social service agency, I think that the report should be disclosed. But, the Rules Committee didn't go that far and they placed it in the discretion of the district judge and some judge disclosed it and others don't.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: I thought, Mr. Glazer, that when petitioner's counsel requested to be released on bond for a short period so that he could visit his family, and that was denied, then petitioner's counsel asked for a presentence report.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: No, he said “I would like to ask that a presentence investigation be made.” He didn't ask to see the report. He asked the Court for -- that the presentence investigation be made. At that point, the --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: He wanted to see it.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: It -- to me, that's the impression I have is he wanted the probation officer to conduct a presentence investigation. He didn't ask the Court to show him --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: You think he should've said that “if a presentence investigation report has been made, I'd like to see it,” even though it's quite obvious that he didn't know that a presentence investigation report had been made.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: He could've said that, right, and, apparently, it --

turns.name: JUSTICE FORTAS
turns.text: I think that's what he had to say.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Apparently, I assume that he assumed that he didn't realize that a report had -- an investigation had commenced. The petitioner's appendix, page 7, it clearly shows what happened here. Mr. Richards: “Your Honor, I would like to ask that a presentence be made of --" The Court interrupting: “A presentence investigation has been made and it is before me now and I have read it.” It says it shows it direct. He makes a -- reads briefly from it in one continuing sentence. And, in three paragraphs, he goes ahead and states briefly what the presentence report says in part and then says “it will be the judgment of this Court that this defendant be sentenced to the mandatory 25 years custody to the martial.” The petitioner was not given the right of allocution after the petitioner was informed that a presentence had been made or that the record was before the Court.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, where were the defendant and his lawyer at the time the judge told them there had been mandate and he read it?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: As I just --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Where were the defendant and his lawyer?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: The defense counsel stated, “Your Honor, I would like to ask that a presentence investigation be made of -- “He was interrupted in the middle of his motion, his request." But then, the Court stated that there one had -- that one had been made --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: But that answered that part of his statement.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: And what did the defendant or his lawyer say?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: The defendant or his lawyer wasn't given the chance to say anything. The Court said that there had been --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: You mean he cut him off?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Yes, he cut him off.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: They couldn't talk?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: The --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Were they standing there before him?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: The Court stated --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Were you his lawyer?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Were you cut off in talk?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Yes, because he had stated it was --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Well, where exactly in here, he first --

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Page 7 of the appendix.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Yes, but he first asked you what did you have to say. “Now, does a defendant or his counsel have anything that they wish to say before sentence is imposed?” On page 6.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: And you did speak.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: And then the Court turned to Mr. Gregg, and Mr. Gregg spoke.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: Right?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: So, where were you cut off?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: When I find out that a presentence investigation had been made, that the Court had read it, and then the Court pronounced sentence.

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: But you -- I don't see where you were cut off.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: “Custody of the martial.”

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: That cut you off.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Maybe if --

turns.name: JUSTICE MARSHALL
turns.text: I see.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: You mean that if you -- if you were to add more to read that -- wanted to see that thing, you wouldn't have asked him?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: At this time, we were so surprised --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, why wouldn't you? Would you let him cut you off that quickly?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: We were so surprised that a presentence investigation had been made --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, that's right, but did you ask him to see it? Why didn't you ask him to see it?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Because the --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Maybe the last paragraph of the colloquy would clear that up.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I've just read it. It didn't clear it up to me.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Richards: “Your Honor, I would like to ask that a presentence investigation be made of --" and then the Court interrupting, “a presentence investigation has been made. It is before me now, and I've read it. It shows the juvenile record. It shows that, in 1960, this defendant stole an automobile in violation of the Tire Act, was given indeterminate youth commitment sentence, his was paroled in 1965, he was returned -- no, he was paroled in 1962, returned as a parole violator in 1965, and was not released full time until May of last year. I am also informed that he was convicted of robbery in Yuma, Arizona and given from 7-10 years. Several warrants are now pending against him for robbery of which he is charged. It will be the judgment of this Court that this defendant be sentenced of the mandatory 25 years, custody of the martial.” To me, that's plain interruption and a lack of opportunity for the counsel even to answer the Court because when he said “custody of the martial,” it was all over.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Do you believe that a lawyer who represents his client from the beginning to the end could be stopped from talking by what was said there?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: At this time --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Would you?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: No.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: He was the lawyer.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Would you stop from talking with what was --

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: It certainly was not, except the defendant --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Did you want to see it?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Did I want to see the presentence investigation?

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: At that time, no. The defendant turned, walked away in the custody of the martial.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: You mean after this colloquy was over?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: Yes. The defendant -- the marital stepped forward. The defendant was taken from the courtroom immediately. Then, we were later brought back and he was advised of his appellate rights some minutes later, 10-15 minutes later, after I had left to get my car started from the parking lot. Then, we were brought back in the courtroom and advised of the appellate rights that the petitioner had.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Well, what were you going to say that you didn't get to say?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: At that time, I had nothing to say, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITE
turns.text: Well, no. When the judge interrupted you there when you asked a presentence report be made, what were you going to say that you didn't get to say?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: I was requesting that a presentence investigation be made. Mr. Gregg -- this was --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: That's something --

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: This was all about --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: That's something which you sought would take a little time and the sentence would be put off until it was completed. That's what you were after, weren't you?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: That's correct, and I was --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: And said of which you discovered, I gather that it's something you didn't know, that it'd already been done so you couldn't get a delay in sentence while pre-investigation.

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: That's correct. I was not going to ask that the report be destroyed --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Are you sure you're not asking a leading question instead of stating what you thought?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: No, Your Honor, I believe I am. Thank you.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Are you leading question or not?

turns.name: SIDNEY M. GLAZER
turns.text: At this time, I was not going to ask. I was only asking that a presentence investigation be made and I was not going to ask that it be disclosed to the petitioner or his counsel. Thank you.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Very well.
